question_text: Which action best completes closed-loop communication after an RN case manager sends a referral-related message?
answer_a: Confirm receipt, clarify the next action, and document the outcome or pending follow-up.
answer_b: Assume the message was handled once it left the RN case manager's inbox.
answer_c: Wait until the next scheduled visit before checking whether the referral was received.
answer_d: Ask the patient to call every partner until someone responds.
rationale: Closed-loop communication requires confirmation and documentation of the response or unresolved barrier; sending a message alone does not prove the patient-care need was addressed.

question_text: Scenario: A fictional patient discharged after repeated falls has no confirmed home therapy evaluation date. What is the best RN case management response?
answer_a: Contact the therapy intake pathway with fall-risk facts, request evaluation timing, confirm receipt, update the patient, and document follow-up.
answer_b: Tell the patient therapy referrals often take time and wait until the next routine call.
answer_c: Document that therapy is pending without contacting anyone because referrals are not nursing concerns.
answer_d: Promise the patient that therapy will arrive the same day.
rationale: The best response links fall risk to the therapy delay, uses closed-loop confirmation, keeps the patient informed, and avoids promising a result outside RN control.

question_text: Which example shows appropriate discipline selection by an RN case manager?
answer_a: Sending medication-access and instruction barriers to the pharmacy and provider while tracking patient safety risk.
answer_b: Sending every problem to the same community agency because it is the easiest contact to reach.
answer_c: Asking the patient to choose which clinician should receive all clinical messages.
answer_d: Waiting to coordinate until every discipline has independently contacted the patient.
rationale: Appropriate discipline selection matches the patient need to the partner who can act while the RN tracks risk and follow-up within scope.

question_text: Which documentation entry best supports continuity after a coordination call?
answer_a: RN contacted pharmacy, confirmed prescription received but provider clarification pending, notified provider office, updated patient, and will recheck by 1500.
answer_c: Patient has medication problem and needs help soon.
answer_d: Will follow up later if time allows.
rationale: The complete entry shows recipient, response, unresolved barrier, patient update, and next follow-up time, allowing another clinician to continue the work.

question_text: Scenario: A fictional patient may miss a wound clinic appointment because transportation failed. Which action best protects coordination?
answer_a: Cancel the appointment until the patient finds transportation independently.
answer_b: Contact the clinic and transportation resource, explain time sensitivity, confirm the revised plan, update the patient, and document the outcome.
answer_c: Tell the patient the wound clinic is responsible for all transportation barriers.
answer_d: Document the missed appointment after it occurs without attempting coordination.
rationale: The correct action coordinates multiple partners, keeps the appointment barrier visible, confirms the plan, and includes the patient before the delay causes a care gap.

question_text: Which provider message is most appropriate for RN case management coordination?
answer_b: Fictional patient reports unclear high-risk medication instructions after discharge; pharmacy needs clarification; please advise today or identify next step.
answer_c: Medication instructions are wrong and must be changed immediately.
answer_d: The RN case manager approved the medication plan after speaking with the patient.
rationale: The best provider message gives concise clinical context, states the barrier, requests a time-sensitive response, and avoids diagnosing or changing orders independently.

question_text: Scenario: A pharmacy says a medication cannot be filled until missing clarification is received. What should the RN case manager do next?
answer_a: Tell the patient to skip the medication until the pharmacy solves it.
answer_b: Notify the appropriate provider channel with pharmacy details, confirm the response plan, update the patient, and document next follow-up.
answer_c: Ask the pharmacy to rewrite the provider's order so the patient can obtain the medication faster.
answer_d: Close the case because pharmacy access issues are outside care coordination.
rationale: The RN coordinates the pharmacy-provider communication loop and tracks patient safety without asking the pharmacy or RN to make a provider-only change.

question_text: Which statement best reflects patient inclusion during interdisciplinary coordination?
answer_a: The RN should coordinate privately so the patient does not need to know what is happening.
answer_b: The RN explains the plan in usable language, confirms contact preferences, reviews what to expect, and documents patient updates.
answer_c: The patient should call each partner daily because patient effort closes the loop.
answer_d: Patient inclusion is only needed after every referral is fully resolved.
rationale: Patient inclusion means the plan is understandable and usable for the patient while the RN continues to manage communication and documentation responsibilities.

question_text: Scenario: A provider office has not responded to a time-sensitive medication clarification request by the expected time. What is the safest RN case management action?
answer_a: Wait several days because provider offices set their own timelines.
answer_b: Mark the issue resolved because the request was sent.
answer_c: Escalate through the appropriate pathway, document the delay and risk, update the patient, and schedule the next follow-up.
answer_d: Ask the patient to change the medication based on symptoms.
rationale: A delayed response that affects medication safety requires escalation and documentation; the RN does not change orders or leave the loop open.

question_text: Which phrase best establishes team accountability in a coordination note?
answer_c: Social work accepted follow-up for caregiver support by next business day; RN will verify status if patient reports no contact.
answer_d: The patient understands that many teams are involved.
rationale: The correct phrase names the owner, action, time frame, and RN verification plan, which makes the next step traceable.

question_text: What information is most important in a safe interdisciplinary handoff?
answer_a: Only the name of the next discipline because the chart contains everything else.
answer_b: A long narrative including every unrelated historical detail.
answer_c: Current status, relevant risk, requested action, urgency, and follow-up ownership.
answer_d: A statement that the RN has finished all responsibility once the message is sent.
rationale: A useful handoff gives the recipient enough focused information to act while preserving accountability for time-sensitive follow-up.

question_text: Scenario: A fictional patient cannot access meals after discharge, which may affect medication tolerance and wound healing. Which RN action is best?
answer_a: Tell the patient community resources are guaranteed once a referral is sent.
answer_b: Document the concern but avoid resource coordination because it is nonclinical.
answer_c: Coordinate an appropriate resource referral, explain eligibility is not guaranteed, assess interim risk, and track the response.
answer_d: Wait for the patient to request help again before taking action.
rationale: The RN links the social barrier to clinical risk, coordinates a resource without overpromising, and tracks the response because the barrier affects care outcomes.

question_text: Scenario: A fictional patient says different team members gave conflicting instructions about follow-up. What is the best RN case management response?
answer_a: Tell the patient to choose whichever instruction is easiest to follow.
answer_b: Ask the patient not to contact the team again until the next appointment.
answer_c: Document confusion but take no action because conflicting instructions are common.
answer_d: Clarify the conflicting instructions with the appropriate partners, provide patient-centered updates within scope, and document the confirmed plan.
rationale: Conflicting instructions create coordination risk; the RN should clarify with accountable partners, include the patient, and document the confirmed plan.

question_text: Which course-status statement is appropriate for this draft module?
answer_a: The module may advertise approval once learners complete the quiz.
answer_b: The certificate can be released after M03 if the learner attests completion.
answer_c: The module should use approval language if the content appears complete.
answer_d: Status remains Draft / Pending BRN CEP Approval, and certificate release waits for provider metadata and completion evidence gates.
rationale: The module must not claim approval or release certificates before provider approval metadata and required completion evidence are available.

question_text: Which measurement statement is correct for M03 learner-facing materials?
answer_a: The module should be labeled by college-credit terminology first.
answer_b: The module should omit time measurement because completion evidence is enough.
answer_c: The module should describe only quiz time because reading time is not relevant.
answer_d: M03 uses contact hours as the primary measurement and targets 3 contact hours.
rationale: The BRN course package uses contact hours as the primary measurement, and M03 is designed for a 3 contact-hour target.
